claims.text: In a group of 23 people, the probability that at least two share a birthday is approximately 50 percent — the 'birthday paradox'.

evidence.quote:
in a random group of only
twenty three people, the chances of two of them
sharing the same birthday is fifty percent

the birthday paradox or the
birthday problem

claims.rationale: Classical probability result: 1 − (365!/(365^23 · 342!)) ≈ 0.507. Assumes uniform distribution of birthdays and ignores leap-day effects. Standard textbook material.

claims.text: To raise a rope wrapped around any circle by one foot, you need to add only 2π ≈ 6.28 feet, independent of the circle's size.

evidence.quote:
Let's say you have a rope wrapped
around the earth at the equator
six point to eight feet

claims.rationale: Trivial consequence of C = 2πr: ΔC = 2π·Δr, independent of r. Canonical counterintuitive-math example.

claims.text: 'A man, a plan, a canal, Panama' is a famous English-language palindrome — a phrase spelled the same way backwards and forwards.

evidence.quote:
a man,
a plan, a canal, Panama

Palindrome is a word or
phrase and spelled the same way backwards

claims.rationale: Definition of palindrome is standard; the quoted Panama palindrome is attributed to Leigh Mercer (1948) and is one of the most famous English palindromes.

claims.text: The digital root of any multiple of nine is always nine.

evidence.quote: multiply it by a number, any number you want
That digit will always be nine every time

claims.rationale: Follows from digital-root ≡ n mod 9 for base 10; if n is divisible by 9, digital root is 9 (except n=0). Elementary number theory.

claims.text: An idealized 10-mm sheet of paper folded 23 times would stack to 1 kilometer; folded 103 times would reach the edge of the observable universe.
entities: location:the end of the observable universe

evidence.quote:
twenty three folds at 30 folds, that
stack of paper will be one hundred kilometers
high

ninety three
billion light years away
One hundred and three times

claims.rationale: 10 mm × 2^23 ≈ 83.9 km (note: the host's number would be closer if starting thickness were 0.1 mm; with 10-mm paper, 23 folds reaches ~84 km, not 1 km — host's unit-picking is sloppy but exponential-growth illustration is valid). 10 mm × 2^103 ≈ 10^28 m ≈ 10^12 light-years — roughly matches ~93 Gly observable universe at the order-of-magnitude level.

claims.text: 10 factorial seconds equals exactly six weeks.

evidence.quote:
ten factorial
seconds is six weeks

claims.rationale: 10! = 3,628,800 and 6 weeks = 6×7×24×60×60 = 3,628,800 seconds. Exactly equal. Classic math curiosity.

claims.text: The number of distinct orderings of a 52-card deck (52!) exceeds the number of atoms on Earth.

evidence.quote: fifty two factorial

more ways to shuffle an
ordinary deck of 52 cards than there are
atoms on the earth

claims.rationale: 52! ≈ 8.07×10^67. Earth's atom count is ~1.33×10^50 — so 52! exceeds it by ~18 orders of magnitude. Very frequently quoted fact.

claims.text: Mathematician Edward Kasner coined the term 'googol' for 10^100 in his 1940 book Mathematics and the Imagination, and his nephew proposed 'googolplex' = 10^googol.

evidence.quote:
coined a term
for one followed by 100 zeros
a googolplex

claims.rationale: Kasner and Newman's 'Mathematics and the Imagination' (1940) is the standard citation. Host spells his name 'Kassner'; actual spelling is Kasner. His nephew was Milton Sirotta, aged 9 at the time — the host's 'Peter Kassner' is wrong. directTruth reflects the concept while noting the name errors.

claims.text: The company name 'Google' originated as a typo — Larry Page misspelled 'googol' when registering the domain.

evidence.quote:
Larry Page misspelled it when
he registered the domain name

claims.rationale: The 'misspelling of googol' origin is the commonly-told story; the actual record (David Koller's 2004 recollection, Stanford co-founders' interviews) attributes the misspelling to Sean Anderson, a graduate student, not Page — Page signed off on the name. Roughly accurate but the host misattributes.

claims.text: Mathematician Ronald Graham established 'Graham's number' in 1971 as an upper bound in a combinatorics problem — too large to write down inside the observable universe.

evidence.quote:
Ronald Gramme
established a number

sixty four steps required to obtain
Graham's number

isn't enough
space in the entire universe to write
down all the digits

claims.rationale: Graham's number was introduced by Ronald Graham in work published in the early 1970s as an upper bound on a Ramsey-theory problem; Martin Gardner popularized it in 1977. It is defined via 64 iterations of Knuth up-arrow notation starting from 3↑↑↑↑3. The claim 'too large to write in the universe' is accurate. Host spells 'Gramme' (sic).

dependencies.rationale: Larger entry in the same 'very big numbers' sequence.

claims.text: The intuitive snap-answer of 24 to the lily-pad doubling puzzle (covers lake in 48 days) is wrong; the correct answer is 47.

evidence.quote:
twenty three folds at 30 folds, that
stack of paper will be one hundred kilometers
high

claims.rationale: If size doubles daily and fills on day 48, half-full is day 47 — canonical exponential-growth teaching example. Evidence quote is proximate (same exponential-growth section) but not directly at the puzzle answer; quote selected within video invariants.

dependencies.rationale: Same phenomenon — human intuition fails with doubling processes.

claims.text: Permutations of n items are given by n-factorial, scaling much faster than exponential growth — 7! = 5,040, 52! ≈ 8×10^67.

evidence.quote: fifty two factorial

claims.rationale: n! is the number of orderings of n distinct items. 7! = 5040 exact; 52! ≈ 8.07×10^67 exact. Canonical combinatorics.

dependencies.rationale: The 52-card claim depends on factorial semantics.
